comments.text: I have resolved this problem here. [EDIT: this version of the manuscript has now been superseded by later version; the latest public version of the manuscript is available here.] The main theorem shows that there exists a minimal asymptotic basis $A\subset\mathbb N$ of order $2$ such that $A(x)=C\sqrt{x}+O(1)$ for a sufficiently large constant $C>0$. Most of the work is my own; however, the end product was achieved with the assistance of GPT 5.5 to stress-test ideas, suggest revisions, and identify possible gaps in earlier versions of this argument. At the moment, some of the proofs and exposition in the manuscript have been written up by GPT. I have personally verified that these correspond to our discussions and take full responsibility for the content.
Congrats! I ran standard check which claimed a few minor issues.
Thanks Nat! I've incorporated those fixes into the latest version of the manuscript.

After weeks of work using Aristotle, Codex, and GPT 5.5, I have now formalised the solution, confirming all of the claims in the manuscript. The full formalisation is currently almost 15,000 lines long! You can type-check it online here. Additionally, here is the latest version of the manuscript. It has not changed significantly, but now incorporates several minor changes needed for completeness (including addressing the minor issues picked up by Nat's standard check).

Thanks. I have not been able to get the linked to Lean formalisation to load yet for me, but I like to try and actually read the proof myself so that I can understand what's going on. I have not yet managed with this proof. I find this AI style of writing proofs very difficult to follow - non-standard terms are introduced with no explanation, important ideas are obscured while trivial observations are spelled out with exhausting detail, and everything is broken down into lemmas labelled with strange 'catch-phrases' that do not really tell me what they are doing, or why they are important. This is a 17 page document about a single construction of a concrete set $A$, but it is difficult to see exactly where in the document this set $A$ is defined. Implicitly it seems to define $A=\cup_i A_i$, but at no point is it clearly explained what the sets $A_i$ actually are. This is not a criticism of the correctness of the proof, either locally or globally - I am sure that if I devoted sufficient time to reading this document I could verify it line by line, and then be able to produce a much clearer sketch of what's going on. But this does not seem to have been done - to use Tao's example, I do not see much evidence that this proof has been 'digested' by any human. (To clarify I am not saying that you definitely haven't, just that I don't see evidence of it here, since the writing style throughout is very similar to the proof writeups that AI generates). I am confused since you say that most of the work is your own, so maybe the faults of the exposition are due to the AI. I encourage you to rewrite the proof (or at least a sketch of the proof) in your own words, to reflect the construction how you see it, how you motivated it in your own mind when going about the construction - try to think what a human reader, who has no idea what you're about to do, will be thinking at each stage. What questions will they have in their mind? What are the most important things they should know about the construction? Are these things easily available early on in the paper? I hope you will forgive me writing at length about this proof - I'm taking this as a single example of a wider trend, and thought it would be interesting/useful to try and clearly pinpoint where this style of writing goes wrong, and why it is much harder to read (at least for me) than almost any human-written proof. Let me take the first paragraph of the 'Idea of the construction' section for example. Now the main result promises a construction of a minimal basis $A$ such that $A(x)\sim cx^{1/2}$ for some $c>0$. This implicitly contains three distinct claims about the set $A$, so what I'd hope for in a 'sketch' section is a summary of (a) how the set $A$ is actually built and (b) why it satisfies those three claims. The italics are from your text; after each sentence I have written what I am thinking, as the reader who is eager to understand this construction. The construction is organised around one active private witness at a time. OK, presumably 'the construction' refers to the set $A$ we're after. I don't know what it means by organised yet, but this suggests some kind of iterative procedure (as typical in these kind of basis constructions), where at each stage there is something called an 'active private witness'. This gives no clue what it refers to (a number? a set?), but presumably this will shortly be made clear. During epoch $j$, a large integer $X_j$ is reserved as the future private witness for a previously constructed element $a_j$. OK, another new non-standard word now, 'epoch'. From context I guess it means the $j$th stage of the construction, so presumably this is referring to how we build $A_j$ having already built $A_1,\ldots,A_{j-1}$ (and then $A=\cup A_j$). (Note that I'm already having to rely on my existing knowledge of similar arguments to make guesses like this.) Now we know what a private witness is - some integer $X_j$. The reason for why it is called a private witness is still mysterious. From the choice of letter I guess that $a_j$ is some element of $\cup_{i<j}A_i$, but I don't know which. Ordinary blocks cover the next range of sums while avoiding all representations of $X_j$. This is very confusing. The next range of sums? Next after what? I guess this means we are trying to satisfy the basis property at this point, so have some interval (presumably, from the use of the word 'range') we are trying to include in $A_{\leq j}+A_{\leq j}$. How this interval is defined I have no idea. I also have no guesses what it means by an 'ordinary block'. Yet another new, non-standard, phrase, which is not explained. And we are meant to avoid all representations of $X_j$. Avoid how? Avoid including any new elements into $A_j$ that, together with some element of $\cup_{i<j}A_i$ (or maybe also including the elements of $A_j$ chosen so far?) produce a pair which sums to $X_j$ perhaps. A lower seam is inserted just below $X_j$, then the release element $b_j = X_j − a_j$ is added, giving the unique representation $X_j = a_j + b_j$. Yet more strange new terminology. What is a 'lower seam'? How is it 'inserted', and into what? Maybe we are removing some elements from the candidate for $A_j$? And then adding $b_j$? (I still have no idea what $a_j$ is.) Smoothing then fills the square shells below $X_j$ to their prescribed capacities while preserving this unique representation. The prefix $\leq X_j$ is then closed permanently, so later epochs cannot create another representation of $X_j$. Finally an upper seam covers the boundary region around $2X_j$. Smoothing? Of what, and how? What is a square shell? Prescribed capacities? What does it mean to 'close a prefix' (which presumably means the closed interval $[1,X_j]$?)? Now there is an 'upper seam' as well? This sketch section continues for a couple more paragraphs, which is more of the same. At no point are any of these strange terms explained in this section. (Or indeed later - I could not see anywhere that an actual definition of what is meant by 'seam' or 'square shell', and it appears one must guess what these refer to by examining the statements of certain lemmas, presuming that the objects produced by these lemmas are what is referred to. If the object of reference of a phrase is not either common knowledge or clearly stated in some easily accessible place, then using the phrase obfuscates more than it clarifies.) I come away after reading this section with no real clue how the set $A$ is constructed, other than some vague ideas about representing something uniquely, and that it is an iterative procedure, constructed to be a basis. I only really know this much because of my pre-existing knowledge about how such constructions usually go. This gives no idea about why this basis is minimal, or why it satisfies the asymptotic claim, or what any of the new terminology introduced (which is used frequently throughout the paper) actually means.

Thanks for your detailed comments. I do agree with your overall sentiment that the AI generated paper is not easily understandable at the moment. For that reason, I have also been working on completely re-writing the manuscript from scratch to make it much more readable. Before posting the final version (which will take at least a few weeks for me to have ready), I will try to ensure that I address all of the issues that you raise here. Over the past few weeks I have primarily looked to formalise the result in Lean and perhaps have not worked on the paper as much as I should have (let me know if you still can't get the formalisation to load and I will try to post it in some other way).

Thanks, I look forward to reading the revised version! (As a dry-run, I strongly recommend explaining the construction in person to another mathematician who hasn't seen the proof before, assuming you can find such a person - this helps a lot in showing you what natural questions a reader might have, which are easy to overlook once you've been thinking about a proof for too long).

Looks like this is false. A counterexample given by GPT-5.4 is $$A = \Bigl\{ \sum_{j\ge 0} \varepsilon_j 3^j : \varepsilon_j\in\{0,1\},\ \text{for all but finitely many j} \Bigr\}$$ Thus $A$ is the set of natural numbers whose ternary digits are all 0 or 1 (alternatively exclude 0 depending on the convention). Here's a short note with a proof that this is indeed a counterexample

I think from context that it was intended that that $\lim_{k\to \infty}a_k/k^2=c\neq 0$ (otherwise there are a number of easy counterexamples such as this one). This is probably better posed as their alternative formulation: can it be the case that\[\lim_{k\to \infty}\frac{a_k}{k^2}=c\neq 0\]if $A=\{a_1<\cdots\}$ is a minimal basis? (Erdős and Graham conjectured no such $A$ exists.) I'll update the site to better reflect this.
Taking a concrete example, If $A$ is the set of squarefree numbers, can one obtain a minimal basis with the required property?
This is not a minimal basis - you can remove any finite collection of even numbers from $A$ and it is still a basis of order $2$.
Yes, a basis just needs to represent all sufficiently large integers, not all integers.
